tiers.name: Part I: Key News & Developments
tiers.description: Macro economy, politics, social security, international relations, industry, and key client movements in Paraguay

tiers.name: Part II: Analysis & Recommendations
tiers.description: Enterprise impact assessment and China-specific strategic recommendations

sections.title: I. Macroeconomic Developments
sections.description: GDP growth, inflation, exchange rate, monetary policy, fiscal position, key industry performance

events.title: Paraguay GDP grew 5.8% in Q1 2026, leading regional growth (2026-06-26)
events.summary: Paraguay's GDP expanded 5.8% YoY in Q1 2026, the highest in Latin America, surpassing Peru (3.5%), Argentina (2.3%), Colombia (2.2%), Brazil (1.8%), Uruguay (0.9%), and Chile (-0.5%). Growth excluding agriculture and binational hydropower reached 6.0%, driven by services (+6.6%), manufacturing (+6.0%), and construction (+5.6%). BCP projects 4.2% growth for full-year 2026.

events.title: BCP holds policy rate at 5.50% for fourth consecutive month amid low inflation (2026-06-25)
events.summary: The Central Bank of Paraguay unanimously held the benchmark rate at 5.50% on June 25, maintaining a neutral stance since February 2026. CPI inflation stands at 2.4% YoY (May 2026), well below the 3.5% target. Core inflation is just 1.21%. The BCP noted declining oil prices (below $80/barrel after a US-Iran temporary deal) as a favorable factor, while citing US tariff uncertainty and geopolitical risks as headwinds.

events.title: IMF recognizes resilience and strength of Paraguay's economy — President Peña (2026-06-26)
events.summary: President Santiago Peña met with IMF officials who acknowledged Paraguay's economic resilience. The government aims to double the economy by 2035. The World Bank projects average growth of 4.2% for 2026–2028. Paraguay is entering its second year with investment grade status from Moody's, with Fitch and S&P upgrades under review.

events.title: Private consumption expanded 4.8% in Q1 2026, BCP reports (2026-06-27)
events.summary: Domestic consumption grew 4.8% YoY in Q1 2026, reflecting strong household demand supported by low inflation (2.4%) and a historically strong guaraní. Gross capital formation rose 3.7%, indicating continued investment momentum. The Paraguayan guaraní was recognized by Bloomberg as the most stable Latin American currency, having appreciated ~17% against the USD in 2025.

sections.title: II. Political & Policy Changes
sections.description: Major legislation, policy shifts, foreign investment regulation, tax reform, investment incentives

events.title: Paraguay launches 'Investor Pass' — direct permanent residency through four investment routes (2026-06-22)
events.summary: Under MIC Resolution 0283/2026 (effective April 28), foreign investors can obtain direct permanent residency through four pathways: (1) Productive investment via SUACE — USD 70,000 minimum, 5 local jobs; (2) Real estate — USD 200,000 in income-generating property; (3) Financial instruments — USD 200,000 held 2+ years; (4) Tourism — USD 150,000. Dividend tax reduced from 15% to 8% for residents. Citizenship available after 3 years without renouncing prior nationality.

events.title: Migration Directorate updates permanent residency economic solvency requirements — effective July 6 (2026-06-25)
events.summary: DNM Resolution 407/2026 establishes 12 categories of economic solvency proof for permanent residency, including professionals, digital nomads, property owners, shareholders, and retirees. A key change: university degrees alone are no longer sufficient — applicants must demonstrate active professional practice and income. The resolution aligns criteria between Law 6984/2022 and the Mercosur Residence Agreement, effective July 6, 2026.

events.title: Presidential decree on Villeta project electricity tariffs cancelled and renegotiated (2026-06-10)
events.summary: A January 2026 presidential decree setting fixed electricity tariffs for ATOME's Villeta green fertilizer project was cancelled and replaced. ANDE has been tasked with negotiating new terms. The project, backed by IFC (World Bank), IDB Invest, and the EIB, would create 4,000 construction jobs and 1,300 operational jobs. The reversal introduces policy uncertainty for energy-intensive foreign investments in Paraguay.

sections.title: III. Social & Labor Conditions
sections.description: Labor market, social stability, public security, employment data

events.title: Minimum wage rises 5% to PYG 3,044,000 effective July 1, 2026 (2026-06-26)
events.summary: The Executive Branch decreed a 5% adjustment to the minimum wage. The new monthly minimum is PYG 3,044,000 (diurnal) and PYG 3,957,200 (nocturnal, with 30% surcharge). Paraguay's minimum wage aligns with the Latin American trend of real increases in 2026, recovering purchasing power lost during the 2021–2024 inflationary period.

events.title: Formal employment hits all-time record — over 842,000 workers registered with IPS (2026-06-27)
events.summary: Chamber of Deputies President Raúl Latorre highlighted that May 2026 saw a historic peak of over 842,000 formal workers registered with the Social Security Institute (IPS). This growth is attributed to macroeconomic strengthening, investment promotion, and industrial expansion policies. Further formalization efforts are acknowledged as an ongoing priority.

events.title: MTESS enables WhatsApp channel for minimum wage violation complaints (2026-06-26)
events.summary: The Ministry of Labor, Employment and Social Security (MTESS) activated a WhatsApp hotline (0993-308-100) for workers to report non-compliance with the new minimum wage. The measure reinforces labor rights enforcement alongside the July 1 wage increase.

events.title: Residency applications surge — projected 80,000 in 2026, driven by Brazilian nationals (2026-06-25)
events.summary: Residency applications in Paraguay have surged from 28,000 (2024) to over 47,000 (2025), with a projection of 80,000 in 2026, primarily driven by Brazilian citizens. The influx reflects Paraguay's growing attractiveness as a destination for migrants, investors, and digital nomads, driven by its territorial tax system and low cost of living.

sections.title: IV. International Relations & Diplomacy
sections.description: Paraguay's relations with major powers, Mercosur dynamics, cross-strait relations, multilateral engagement

events.title: Mercosur Summit in Asunción (June 29–30) to decide EU quota distribution — Paraguay demands 25% share (2026-06-26)
events.summary: The LXVII Mercosur Summit will be held June 29–30 in Asunción with the EU trade quota distribution as the central issue. Paraguay demands a fixed 25% share per product category, while Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay favor distribution based on historical export averages. The EU-Mercosur FTA entered provisional force on May 1, 2026, pending European Parliament ratification. Uruguay assumes the pro tempore presidency on June 30.

events.title: Taiwan Deputy Foreign Minister visits Paraguay, trilateral US-Taiwan-Paraguay cooperation explored (2026-06-18)
events.summary: Taiwan's Deputy Foreign Minister Chen Ming-chi visited Paraguay (June 16–19), meeting with FM Rubén Ramírez Lezcano. They reviewed progress following President Peña's May 2026 state visit to Taiwan and discussed a trilateral US-Taiwan-Paraguay cooperation framework on AI, 5G, smart transportation, and cybersecurity. Both sides are preparing a commemorative agenda for the 70th anniversary of diplomatic relations in 2027.

events.title: Paraguay FM vows to maintain Taiwan ties, resisting Beijing's pressure (2026-06-22)
events.summary: Foreign Minister Ramírez Lezcano reaffirmed that Paraguay will not accept China's conditions to sever ties with Taiwan. Paraguay remains the only South American country with full diplomatic relations with Taiwan (1 of 12 globally). Cooperation pillars include the Taiwan-Paraguay Polytechnic University (UPTP), HIS health system deployed in 1,000+ facilities, and the Asunción General Hospital construction.

events.title: Paraguay strengthens alignment with US — SOFA agreement signed, participation in Shield of the Americas summit (2026-06-22)
events.summary: Paraguay has deepened its strategic alignment with the United States, signing a Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA) and participating in the 'Shield of the Americas' summit. Analysts note this alignment creates a triangular Taipei-Washington-Asunción dynamic that may complicate Paraguay's long-term diplomatic strategy if US policy on Taiwan shifts under the Trump administration.

events.title: EU-Mercosur FTA in provisional force since May 1 — Uruguay assumes Mercosur presidency (2026-06-27)
events.summary: The EU-Mercosur trade agreement entered provisional force on May 1, 2026, pending final ratification by the European Parliament after an ECJ compatibility ruling. Argentina already exhausted preferential quotas for honey, eggs, and rice within weeks. Brazil's poultry exports to the EU surged 61.6% in May. Paraguay has begun bilateral quota-based trade under the agreement.

sections.title: V. Industry Focus: Telecom, Digital Energy & AI
sections.description: 5G spectrum and commercial rollout, fiber optic infrastructure, digital energy investment, AI data center development, industry regulation

events.title: CONATEL opens public consultation for 2.3 GHz and 3.5 GHz spectrum tender — key to 5G expansion (2026-06-22)
events.summary: CONATEL launched a public consultation (Resolution 790/2026, April 14) on the tender terms for 2,300 MHz and 3,500 MHz spectrum bands for mobile broadband and 5G services. Comments were accepted through May 15. The final tender document is expected in H2 2026. Separately, CONATEL Resolution 3339/2025 redesignated the 3,700–3,800 MHz band for IMT (5G NR) use, paving the way for expanded 5G deployment.

events.title: HIVE Digital Technologies launches AI BUZZ Cloud platform in Paraguay — Tier III data center with 300MW renewable energy (2026-06-24)
events.summary: HIVE Digital Technologies (NASDAQ: HIVE) launched one of Paraguay's first purpose-built AI cloud platforms, hosted at a Tier III data center in Asunción in partnership with the country's leading telecom operator. The platform leverages Paraguay's national dark fiber network and 300MW of surplus hydroelectric power (Itaipú/Yacyretá). A 100MW expansion is planned for 2026. The GPU cluster will support AI training, inference, and data-intensive workloads for clients across South America.

events.title: Nubicom announces $200M investment for largest 5G deployment in Paraguay (2026-06-23)
events.summary: Argentine operator Nubicom announced a $200 million investment plan for 5G network deployment in Paraguay, the largest single 5G investment in the country. Nubicom won 200 MHz in the 3.3–3.5 GHz band at the August 2025 CONATEL auction ($2M license fee) and is building a mobile network from scratch, having been an Argentine fixed-internet provider without prior mobile infrastructure. Commercial launch is targeted for Q1–Q2 2026 with a minimum of 100 base stations per 100 MHz.

events.title: Claro wins 200 MHz of 3.5 GHz spectrum, set to launch commercial 5G in Greater Asunción (2026-06-23)
events.summary: Claro (América Móvil) won 200 MHz in the 3.5–3.7 GHz band at the CONATEL 5G auction, paying ~$2 million. Commercial 5G launch is expected in Greater Asunción by Q2 2026 with a six-month deployment obligation. Claro confirmed its mobile core network has already been upgraded for 5G. Tigo and Personal withdrew from the auction due to restrictions on Chinese equipment vendors (Huawei, ZTE), a move publicly endorsed by the US Embassy.

sections.title: VI. Key Client Movements
sections.description: Tracking strategic moves, investment plans, network deployment, and technology choices of Paraguay's major telecom clients

events.title: Claro — Wins 5G spectrum, prepares commercial launch in Asunción; core network upgraded (2026-06-23)
events.summary: Claro (América Móvil) secured 200 MHz of 3.5 GHz spectrum and is targeting commercial 5G launch in Greater Asunción by Q2 2026. Its mobile core has been upgraded for 5G. Deployment obligation: 100 base stations per 100 MHz, with 10% serving government entities. Claro is poised to be the first major operator offering 5G services in Paraguay.

events.title: Tigo — Retains 4G market leadership; limited 5G in select Asunción zones; withdrew from 5G auction (2026-06-23)
events.summary: Tigo (Millicom) remains the largest mobile operator in Paraguay by subscribers with the fastest 4G LTE network and best rural coverage. However, Tigo withdrew from the August 2025 5G spectrum auction due to restrictions on Chinese equipment vendors. Its current 5G footprint is limited to select zones in Asunción using existing spectrum. Company continues investing in 4G infrastructure and Tigo Money fintech services.

events.title: Personal — No 5G plans announced; focused on low-cost niche; coverage gaps persist (2026-06-23)
events.summary: Personal (Telecom Argentina) chose not to participate in the 5G auction and has not announced any independent 5G deployment plans. The operator continues to focus on its low-cost market niche with coverage concentrated in urban areas and weaker presence outside Asunción. It risks being displaced by Nubicom as the third mobile operator if it fails to upgrade its network.

events.title: Nubicom — New market entrant with $200M 5G investment; building mobile network from scratch (2026-06-23)
events.summary: Nubicom, an Argentine fixed-internet provider from Salta province, won 200 MHz of 3.5 GHz spectrum and plans a $200 million greenfield 5G network deployment in Paraguay. It must establish a local subsidiary (6-week deadline), build mobile infrastructure from zero, and deploy at least 100 base stations per 100 MHz. This represents the largest single 5G investment in Paraguay.

sections.title: VII. Comprehensive Enterprise Impact Analysis
sections.description: Synthesizing macro trends, industry dynamics, and client movements to assess enterprise operating environment in Paraguay
sections.impact_analysis:
## I. Cost Dimension 🟡

Comprehensive assessment of cost pressures based on this week's data:

- **Energy costs**: Presidential decree cancellation on Villeta project electricity tariffs introduces policy uncertainty for energy-intensive investments. However, Paraguay's abundant hydroelectric surplus (300MW+ available) and HIVE's successful 300MW data center deal confirm energy remains a structural cost advantage.
- **Exchange rate costs**: The guaraní remains Latin America's most stable currency (Bloomberg), having appreciated ~17% vs USD in 2025. The free market rate is approximately 6,124 PYG/USD (June 28). Import-intensive businesses benefit from strong currency; exporters face margin pressure.
- **Labor costs**: The 5% minimum wage increase (PYG 3,044,000 from July 1) is moderate and aligned with regional trends. Formal employment at a record 842,000+ workers signals a tightening but still affordable labor market.

## II. Market Dimension 🟢

Paraguay's market opportunities are expanding notably in digital infrastructure:

- **5G & Telecom**: CONATEL's new spectrum tender (2.3/3.5 GHz) and the Claro/Nubicom 5G rollout create opportunities for telecom equipment, services, and application providers. The US-backed equipment restrictions favor non-Chinese vendors.
- **AI & Data Centers**: HIVE's AI cloud platform launch positions Paraguay as an emerging AI infrastructure hub in South America, leveraging renewable energy and dark fiber. This opens demand for data center construction, cooling, power management, and skilled IT labor.
- **EU Market Access**: The EU-Mercosur FTA (provisional force since May 1) opens preferential quotas for beef, poultry, sugar, and other products. Paraguay's demand for a 25% quota share reflects ambition to capture export growth.

## III. Compliance Dimension 🔴

Compliance risks require active monitoring:

- **Foreign investment policy**: The Investor Pass (MIC Resolution 0283/2026) and updated DNM Resolution 407/2026 substantially improve residency pathways but add new documentation requirements (active income proof). The rapid surge in applications (80,000 projected for 2026) may strain processing capacity.
- **Energy regulation**: The Villeta decree reversal signals that energy-intensive projects cannot assume fixed tariff stability. ANDE renegotiation introduces execution timeline risk.
- **Labor compliance**: MTESS WhatsApp complaint channel signals increased enforcement of minimum wage and labor standards. Companies should audit payroll compliance ahead of the July 1 wage adjustment.

## Integrated Assessment

This week's 22 monitored events (7 government-sourced) paint a picture of strong macroeconomic momentum (GDP +5.8%, record employment) combined with policy fluidity in energy and foreign investment regulation. The key near-term decision point is the June 29–30 Mercosur Summit outcome on EU quota distribution, which will shape Paraguay's export competitiveness for years.

sections.title: VIII. China-Specific Enterprise Risk & Opportunity Analysis
sections.description: Tailored analysis for Chinese enterprises operating in or considering Paraguay, covering diplomatic risk, policy risk, and strategic recommendations
sections.impact_analysis:
## I. Risk Alerts

🔴 **High Priority:**

- **Diplomatic barrier**: Paraguay remains Taiwan's sole South American diplomatic ally. FM Ramírez Lezcano publicly reaffirmed refusal to switch recognition to Beijing. SOFA agreement with the US and trilateral US-Taiwan-Paraguay cooperation (AI, 5G, cybersecurity) further entrench this alignment. Chinese enterprises face elevated political scrutiny in project approvals and may encounter informal barriers.
- **Equipment vendor exclusion**: CONATEL's 5G auction rules effectively barred Chinese telecom equipment vendors (Huawei, ZTE) on security grounds, with explicit US Embassy endorsement. This precedent could extend to other critical infrastructure sectors, limiting market access for Chinese technology exports.

🟡 **Medium Priority:**

- **Competitive displacement**: Claro (América Móvil) and Nubicom ($200M investment) are capturing the 5G market first-mover advantage while Chinese vendors are excluded. Tigo and Personal, both Huawei/ZTE users, face strategic challenges in upgrading their networks.
- **Exchange rate exposure**: The strong guaraní (appreciated ~17% in 2025) reduces the CNY value of PYG-denominated profits for Chinese investors already in Paraguay.

## II. Opportunity Analysis

🟢 **Actionable Opportunities:**

- **Digital infrastructure gap**: Despite vendor restrictions, the HIVE AI data center project demonstrates demand for digital infrastructure partnerships. Chinese firms could explore non-equipment roles: engineering procurement construction (EPC), renewable power integration, cooling systems, and data center operations.
- **Agricultural technology**: Paraguay's soybean and beef export boom (GDP +5.8%, agriculture +8.2%) creates demand for precision agriculture, IoT sensors, and supply chain digitization — areas where Chinese agritech firms can compete without triggering security concerns.
- **Mercosur gateway**: Chinese enterprises with existing Brazil or Argentina operations can leverage the EU-Mercosur FTA framework for regional market access. The June 29–30 Mercosur Summit outcome on EU quotas may create new export channel opportunities.

## III. Strategic Recommendations

**Immediate:**
- Map all existing Paraguay investments against the new Investor Pass and DNM 407/2026 residency requirements to ensure compliance.
- Assess whether any telecom or critical infrastructure projects involve Chinese-origin equipment that could be affected by CONATEL's vendor restrictions.

**Short-term (1–3 months):**
- Explore third-country partnership models (e.g., via European or Brazilian subsidiaries) for 5G, data center, and digital infrastructure projects to mitigate political sensitivity.
- Establish a local policy monitoring mechanism to track CONATEL spectrum tenders, ANDE energy tariff negotiations, and MIC investment regulation changes.

**Medium-term (3–12 months):**
- Monitor the US-Taiwan-Paraguay trilateral framework evolution and its impact on Chinese enterprise access.
- Evaluate agricultural technology and renewable energy as lower-political-risk entry points for the Paraguayan market.
